text: her face. Her eyes close again, and I listen to the soft rhythm of how she breathes when she’s sleeping. Even so, I hold off on homework and read Charlotte’s Web to her until Mom calls me for dinner. It’s always been Della’s favorite book. # It’s the third Saturday of the month and we’re all supposed to go to Gramma’s house for the monthly pilgrimage. Della was awake twice during the night, which means I was too. Over the last few weeks, I’ve turned into a human tripwire, on alert most of the night, listening for the splash of vomit in the now-permanent fixture of a bucket by Della’s bed. Her eyes are closed when I get up, and while she’s sleeping, I take a good look at her. When Mrs. Eckenrode was done drawing deformed penises on the white board, she talked about mushroom spores and how they flourish . Whatever the opposite of flourish is, Della is exactly that. Her skin, somehow always blemish-free and the shade of highly-creamed coffee, is now the color of the pages in an old paperback novel. Beneath her eyes, there are darkened shapes of crescent moons turned on their side. Even her hair has changed, the thick waves shrunken somehow to thin strands that cling together. I want to mess with her. Stick the tip of my index finger up one of her nostrils, or run my fingertips, spiderweb softly, against her ear. I want to mess with her and watch her open her eyes and swat my hand away as she giggles herself awake. But I don’t. I put on jeans and a shirt my parents will say is acceptable for a visit to Gramma’s house. Della doesn’t bat an eyelid as I finish dressing, and I stop and stare at her again. When I leave, and softly shut the door behind me, I feel like I’m leaving her alone at the bottom of the pool at Twin Oaks. # Mom stays with Della, and it’s only Dad and I on the drive. I stay off my phone as he doesn’t like when I’m on it and it’s only the two of us. Says he feels like he’s being ignored . Not that it matters much today, as Dad’s humming along to the music on a classic rock station and not talking to me anyway. As he drives, I study his facial expressions from the corner of my eye. He looks distracted. I wonder what he’s thinking about because every once in a while, his eyebrows knit together like he’s remembering something painful. Mom’s been wearing the same kind of expression lately, which is unusual. It stands out because my mother is normally what you’d call nondescript. She doesn’t stand out at all. Ever. That sounds harsh until you meet her. Her style is no style. I’ve been wondering what she’s been thinking too, but I’m not going to ask. Not her, or my dad. No one asks your parents that kind of thing. I know they love us. I mean, obviously. Dad’s still taking me to Gramma’s house to visit, and Mom is taking care of Della back home. Of course , they love us. There are moments when I’m just not sure either one of them likes us much. # Because she asked me to, I sit on Della’s bed and try to help her with Applied Economics homework. I hear her sigh in frustration, plop her head down on her pillow. Both of us know this isn’t gonna happen right now. “It’s okay.” Della doesn’t reply and when I see her eyes are glassy, borderline crying, I don’t say anything either. Right now, I’m not sure if there’s anything I can say to help. She’s not getting better. I glance at the prescription bottles on the nightstand. I want to scream at them for not doing their job, but I reach out and give Della’s shoulder a soft squeeze. I reach into my backpack and pull out a small stack of Get Well Soon cards from people at school. I can’t help but notice the amount is getting fewer from only a couple weeks ago, and when I put the pile on the nightstand by the pill bottles, I see Della look at them. I can tell she notices, too. Della ignores the cards and so I lay down beside her on my back and start watching stupid videos on my phone. After a few minutes, she rolls over and joins me. It feels good, her being beside me like this, and though she doesn’t laugh, I hear her snicker several times. For right now, that’s enough. Every once in a while, I find myself staring at her for too long, studying her as if she was some sort of sculpture. She catches me sometimes, but Della never says anything. I see this quick expression drift over her face, and then she nestles down onto the bed and pulls the quilt up close. On the nights she catches me staring, I leave my own bed and lay beside her. Part of me knows how bad this really is. Part of me is trying to memorize her. I want to wrap her up and save her. I dream on those nights. Always, the same dream of being in the deep end of the pool and how it feels sitting there with all that water around me, waiting for my sister to return from the surface, the land of the living, and breathe life into my lungs. In my dreams, she swims away and never comes back down. I always wake up sweating, clawing at the empty air above my bed. # I am a mixture of anger and disgust as I march upstairs. Right after school dismissal, Gavin Bowers asked me to go with him to the Spring Dance. Admittedly, him talking to me made the nest of tummy snakes inside me coil up into a tight writhing
